ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn entry for Chinese BBQ stir-fried/grilled beef; FOODON_03311737 (beef dish) used as closest defensible parent class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00LC (beef meat, cooked) used as closest defensible match; no specific code for Chinese BBQ stir-fried beef exists in the FoodEx2 catalogue.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 226934003 (beef dish) used as closest defensible match for a Chinese-style cooked beef preparation.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of Chinese BBQ beef weighs approximately 250 g, representing a typical single-person serving at a Chinese restaurant or hawker stall in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Beef, short loin, top loin steak, boneless, separable lean and fat, trimmed to 1/8 inch fat, all grades, cooked, grilled (FDC ID 174033); adapted with soy-sauce and oyster-sauce seasoning adjustment for sodium and carbohydrate
nutrient_source.organization: U.S. Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA FoodData Central grilled beef (FDC ID 174033) used as the primary nutrient anchor for protein, fat, and energy. Sodium (480 mg/100 g) and carbohydrate (6.0 g/100 g) values are elevated above plain grilled beef due to soy sauce and oyster sauce seasoning typical of Chinese BBQ preparations; these values are consistent with the input record and are plausible for this dish type. Singapore HPB Foodo database consulted as secondary reference for Chinese-style beef dishes in the Singapore context.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition; beef and mixed meat dishes with soy-based sauces analogous estimate
gi_source.organization: American Journal of Clinical Nutrition (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Chinese BBQ stir-fried or grilled beef. GI of 45 is an analogous estimate based on mixed meat dishes with soy-based sauces reported in Atkinson et al. (2021) international GI tables. The low GI reflects the dominant protein and fat content with modest carbohydrate from sauces. GL per serving = (45 × 6.0 × 2.5) / 100 = 6.75 / 2.5 = 2.7 per 250 g serving.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 480 mg per 100 g from soy sauce and oyster sauce
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 100–150 g and request low-sodium soy sauce substitution, as a standard 250 g serving delivers approximately 1200 mg sodium, representing 52% of the WHO daily limit of 2300 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low carbohydrate (6.0 g/100 g) and high protein (14.0 g/100 g) moderating postprandial glucose response
type2_diabetes.guidance: This dish is suitable for people with Type 2 Diabetes in moderate portions; the low carbohydrate content (15 g per 250 g serving) and high protein slow gastric emptying and blunt postprandial glucose rise, though patients should monitor sauce quantity as oyster sauce and soy sauce contribute refined carbohydrate and sodium.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat (4.0 g/100 g), trans fat (0.2 g/100 g), and high sodium (480 mg/100 g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume this dish occasionally and in small portions (100–150 g), as a 250 g serving provides 10 g saturated fat and 1200 mg sodium, both of which contribute to atherogenic risk and blood pressure elevation; choosing leaner cuts and reducing sauce volume will lower the cardiovascular burden.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Red meat arachidonic acid content promoting pro-inflammatory cytokine production (TNF-alpha, IL-6)
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should limit or avoid this dish, as beef is a rich source of arachidonic acid (omega-6) that amplifies TNF-alpha and IL-6 inflammatory pathways, and the high sodium (480 mg/100 g) may worsen RA-related tissue oedema.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Chinese BBQ Beef (中式烧牛肉) is a popular Chinese-style cooked beef dish served in Singapore's Chinese restaurants and hawker centres. The beef — typically flank, sirloin, or short loin cuts — is marinated in a blend of soy sauce, oyster sauce, sesame oil, ginger, garlic, and a small amount of sugar before being stir-fried at high heat in a wok or grilled over charcoal or a gas flame. The result is tender, slightly caramelised beef with a savoury-sweet umami flavour profile and a lightly charred exterior. The dish is served as a main protein component alongside steamed rice or noodles. Nutritionally, per 100 g it provides approximately 180 kcal, 14 g protein, 11.2 g fat (of which 4.0 g is saturated), 6.0 g carbohydrate, and 480 mg sodium. The high sodium content from soy and oyster sauces makes it unsuitable for individuals with hypertension unless sauce quantities are reduced. The low carbohydrate and high protein profile make it suitable for Type 2 Diabetes management in moderate portions. The saturated fat and trans fat content (0.2 g/100 g) warrant caution for cardiovascular disease patients. The dish is not suitable for vegetarians or vegans and contains soy and gluten allergens from the sauces used in preparation.
common_user_queries: How many calories in Chinese stir-fried beef?
Can diabetics eat Chinese BBQ beef?
Is Chinese grilled beef high in sodium?
What is 牛肉 in English?
Is stir-fried beef suitable for hypertension?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central grilled beef (FDC ID 174033) with plausible adjustments for soy sauce and oyster sauce seasoning. No exact Singapore HPB Foodo entry for this specific Chinese BBQ beef dish was identified; values are consistent with the input record and defensible for this dish type. GI is an analogous estimate from peer-reviewed international GI tables. Confidence is medium rather than high due to the adapted rather than exact source match.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, B-vitamin panel, and moisture data for this specific Chinese BBQ stir-fried or grilled beef preparation are not available in USDA FoodData Central (FDC ID 174033 adapted record) or the Singapore HPB Foodo database at the dish level as of 2024. trans_fat_g is reported as 0.2 g/100 g consistent with the input record and plausible for a beef dish with minor natural trans fats (vaccenic acid) from ruminant fat. GI of 45 is an analogous estimate from Atkinson et al. (2021) international GI tables; no direct GI measurement for this dish exists. GL per 250 g serving = (45 × 6.0 × 2.5) / 100 = 2.7. Halal status is marked false as preparation method and sauce sourcing cannot be confirmed from available data; consumers should verify with the restaurant. The input source URL (https://foodo.sg/) was reviewed but does not constitute a primary nutrient data source; USDA FoodData Central was used as the primary approved source.
